stepAnswer: No because tuning k on the test set means you're overfitting to that specific test data, and the accuracy won't reflect $$real-world$$ performance.
stepTitle: e) Suppose Dagny obtains a test set consisting of $$50$$ data points. Should she repeatedly use her classifier on the test set, using various values of k, to obtain the value of k that yields the greatest accuracy? Explain.
choices: Yes because trying all values of k and picking the best one is good practice for maximizing accuracy.
Yes because as long as the test set is large enough, repeatedly testing different k values won't cause any issues due to independence.
No because tuning k on the test set means you're overfitting to that specific test data, and the accuracy won't reflect $$real-world$$ performance.
No because you should never evaluate your classifier on the test set at all.
